title: Why the return of laughter does not mean the pain was not real
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

 I was sitting on the couch with my partner, and they said something stupid. Not mean stupid. Just dumb joke stupid....
metaDescription:
I laughed today. It caught me off guard. 

 I was sitting on the couch with my partner, and they said something stupid. Not mean stupid. Just dumb joke st...
htmlContent:
I laughed today. It caught me off guard.

I was sitting on the couch with my partner, and they said something stupid. Not mean stupid. Just dumb joke stupid. And I laughed. A real laugh. The kind that comes from your gut and leaves you breathless for a second. And then I stopped. My body went cold. Because here's the thing: I had spent the entire morning writing about trauma. About the way my nervous system still braces for impact years after the abuse ended. About the nights I can't sleep because my brain keeps replaying scenes I'd rather forget. And then I laughed. How dare I.

That's the lie, right? The lie we've all been sold. That laughter and pain cannot coexist. That if you're still healing, you shouldn't be happy yet. That the return of joy somehow invalidates the suffering that came before. I've had clients tell me they feel guilty for smiling. For enjoying a meal. For feeling anything other than the weight of their history. And I get it. I really do. Because when you've spent years surviving, pleasure feels like betrayal.

But here's what I've learned, slowly, painfully, over time: your nervous system doesn't work that way. It doesn't keep a ledger of suffering and joy. It doesn't say "you've laughed enough today, time to feel the pain again." It's more complex than that. And more generous.

I want to talk about this because I think it's one of the most misunderstood parts of recovery. The idea that healing means you'll eventually stop hurting. That one day you'll wake up and the pain will be gone and you'll just be... fine. That's not how it works. That's not how any of this works.

What the research actually says about trauma and joy

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, would tell you that your nervous system is constantly scanning for safety. He calls it neuroception. It's happening below your conscious awareness. Your body is always asking: am I safe? Can I rest? Can I connect? Or do I need to fight, flee, or freeze?

When you've experienced complex trauma, your neuroception gets stuck. It's like a smoke alarm that won't turn off. Every sound, every look, every silence feels like a threat. Your body stays in survival mode because it learned, through years of experience, that safety was an illusion.

But here's the wild part. Your nervous system is also capable of what Porges calls "the social engagement system." When you feel truly safe, your body can access states of connection, play, and joy. Not because the trauma is gone. But because your nervous system is flexible enough to experience both. Stay with me here.

I remember reading about a study where researchers measured cortisol levels in trauma survivors who participated in laughter therapy. Their stress hormones dropped. Not because they were "cured." But because laughter activates the vagus nerve. It signals to your body that you're safe enough to let your guard down. Even for a moment.

That doesn't mean the trauma wasn't real. It means your body is doing exactly what it's supposed to do. It's responding to the present moment. And in this moment, right now, you're not in danger. You're on a couch. You're with someone who loves you. You're laughing at a stupid joke. That's not betrayal. That's survival. That's your system finally getting a break.

The guilt of getting better

I've worked with survivors who told me they stopped therapy because they started feeling better. They couldn't handle it. The improvement felt wrong. Like they were abandoning their past selves. Like they were saying "it wasn't that bad" by allowing themselves to feel good.

This is so common, and it breaks my heart every time. Because the truth is, your ability to feel joy is not a measure of how much you suffered. It's a measure of how much you've healed. And healing is not linear. It's not a straight line from pain to peace. It's more like a spiral. You come back to the same places, but you're different each time. You're more resourced. You have more tools. You can hold more.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about pendulation. The idea that healing involves moving between states of activation and settling. You feel the fear. You feel the grief. And then you feel the relief. The joy. The laughter. Not because you're done. But because that's how the nervous system works. It oscillates. It expands and contracts. It's alive.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on the inner critic and emotional flashbacks has helped so many people understand why they feel guilty for feeling good. He names it. He normalizes it. And he gives you a way through it.

I've had days where I felt great. Productive. Connected. And then I'd get hit with a wave of shame so intense I'd have to lie down. What right do I have to feel this good? Who do I think I am? The answer, I've learned, is that I'm a human being with a nervous system that's trying to heal. And sometimes healing looks like laughter. Sometimes it looks like tears. Sometimes it looks like both in the same hour.

When the return of laughter feels like a betrayal

I want to name something specific here. Something that doesn't get talked about enough. There's a particular kind of guilt that comes when you start laughing again after years of silence. It's not just about feeling better. It's about who you used to be. The version of you that couldn't laugh. That couldn't imagine a world where joy existed. That version of you is still inside. And when you laugh, it can feel like you're leaving them behind.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helped me understand why I felt responsible for everyone else's feelings. Why my joy felt like a burden. Why I thought I had to stay small and quiet and sad to keep the peace. That book showed me that my laughter was never the problem. The problem was that I was raised to believe I didn't deserve it.

Here's what I want you to hear, and I need you to really hear this: your laughter does not erase your pain. It doesn't make the abuse less real. It doesn't mean you're "over it." It means you're alive. And being alive means feeling a range of things. Sometimes in the same breath.

I have a friend who survived years of domestic violence. She told me once that the hardest part of recovery wasn't the flashbacks or the nightmares. It was the first time she laughed at a party and realized she hadn't thought about him for three hours. She panicked. She thought she was forgetting. She thought she was betraying herself. But here's the thing: she wasn't forgetting. She was healing. Her nervous system was finally getting a break from hypervigilance. That's not betrayal. That's liberation.

Think about it. If you spend your entire life bracing for impact, your body gets exhausted. Your muscles stay tight. Your breath stays shallow. Your mind stays alert. That's not sustainable. Eventually, something has to give. And when it does, when you finally feel safe enough to let go, that's not weakness. That's your system saying "I trust this moment." And that's huge.

The wellness industry got this wrong

I need to be direct about this. The wellness industry has done a lot of damage with the idea that you have to "process" your trauma completely before you can experience joy. That you have to sit in the pain until it's fully resolved. That laughter is somehow a distraction from "real" healing.

That's not how the nervous system works. That's not how trauma recovery works. And honestly, it's a form of toxic positivity in reverse. It's saying you're not allowed to feel good until you've suffered enough. That's not healing. That's punishment.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She writes about the importance of reconnection and rebuilding a life after trauma. Not just surviving. Not just processing. But actually living. Finding pleasure. Finding meaning. Finding laughter. That's part of recovery. It's not a bonus. It's essential.

I've had clients who felt pressured to stay in their pain by therapists who believed that laughter was a form of avoidance. And I understand the concern. Sometimes we do use humor to deflect. Sometimes we laugh because we can't handle the truth. But that's different. That's a coping mechanism, not a sign of healing.

The difference is in your body. When you're laughing from a place of genuine connection, your body feels open. Relaxed. Safe. When you're laughing to avoid pain, your body feels tight. Guarded. Your laugh might sound hollow. You might feel drained afterward. Pay attention to that. Your body knows the difference.

What to do when the guilt shows up

If you've felt that guilt, that shame, that fear after laughing, here's what I want you to try. Notice what just happened in your body. Right now. As you read this. Where do you feel the tension? Is it in your chest? Your throat? Your stomach? Just notice it. Don't try to change it. Don't try to fix it. Just let it be there.

Now, I want you to think about the last time you laughed. Really laughed. Not a polite laugh. A real one. What did it feel like in your body? Maybe your shoulders dropped. Maybe your jaw relaxed. Maybe you felt a warmth in your chest. That's your nervous system saying "I'm safe." That's not a betrayal of your past. That's a gift to your present.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Because here's the thing: the guilt you feel about laughing is often a boundary issue. You're not sure you're allowed to feel good. You're not sure you deserve it. And that's a boundary you need to set with yourself. You are allowed to feel joy. Full stop. No conditions. No prerequisites.

I'm not saying you'll never feel pain again. You will. That's part of being human. But the pain and the joy can coexist. They don't cancel each other out. They're both real. They're both valid. And they're both part of your story.

So laugh. Laugh until your stomach hurts. Laugh until you cry. And when the guilt shows up, thank it for trying to protect you. Then let it go. Because you don't need to be protected from joy. You need to be protected from the belief that you don't deserve it.

The return of laughter doesn't mean the pain wasn't real. It means you're still here. It means you're still fighting. It means your nervous system is finally learning that not every moment is a threat. And that, my friend, is the whole point.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
